1: katanoeisate ton apostolon kai archierea tēs homologias hēmōn Iēsoun — "consider Jesus, the apostle and high priest of our confession" (katanoeisate: aorist imperative of katanoeō, to observe carefully, to fix one's attention on — the kata intensifies the observation): Jesus is called apostolos (apostle, sent one) only here in the NT — the term that the community uses for its own commissioned messengers is applied to the Son as the original sent-one from the Father. Homologias (confession): the common confession of faith that the community shares and that defines them.
6: Christos de hōs huios epi ton oikon autou — "but Christ is faithful over God's house as a Son" (epi ton oikon: over the house — the preposition epi with accusative indicates authority over): the Son's position is qualitatively different from Moses's en (within) the house — the Son is over it as the builder and heir. Hou oikos esmen hēmeis (whose house we are) — the community is identified as God's household; the ecclesiology is household-shaped. Eanper tēn parrēsian kai to kauchēma tēs elpidos mechri telous bebaian katáschōmen — "if indeed we hold fast our confidence and our boasting in our hope firm to the end."
13: alla parakaleite heautous kath' hekastēn hēmeran, achris hou to Sēmeron kaleitai, hina mē sklērunthē tis ex hymōn apatē tēs hamartias — "but exhort one another every day, as long as it is called today, that none of you may be hardened by the deceitfulness of sin" (apatē tēs hamartias: the deceitfulness of sin — apatē is deceit, delusion; sin is personified as a deceiver who leads the mind astray from reality): sklērunthē (be hardened — aorist passive subjunctive of sklērunō): hardening is a process that daily mutual exhortation resists. The "today" of Ps 95 is the present moment of the community's hearing — a kairos that closes when the heart hardens.
14: metochoi gar tou Christou gegonamen, eanper tēn archēn tēs hypostaseōs mechri telous bebaian katáschōmen — "for we have come to share in Christ, if indeed we hold our original confidence firm to the end" (metochoi tou Christou: partakers/sharers of Christ — metochos appears 6x in Hebrews; sharing/participation is a key Hebrews concept): hypostaseōs — the same word as in 1:3 (charakter tēs hypostaseōs); here it means the foundational confidence/assurance. The sharing in Christ is conditioned on maintaining the beginning (archēn) of that assurance to the end — the condition is not soteriological uncertainty but the community's perseverance in the faith-life.
2: piston onti tō poiēsanti auton, hōs kai Mōysēs en holō tō oikō autou — "He was faithful to the one who appointed him, just as Moses was faithful in all God's house" (pistos: faithful, trustworthy — G4103): the comparison with Moses is the tertium comparationis — faithfulness. The ground-point is the same (faithfulness to the one who appointed); the contrast will be the position (v5-6: servant vs. son). Tō poiēsanti auton (to the one who made/appointed him — poieō here carries the sense of appointment/commissioning, not creation): the Son's faithfulness is to his vocational appointment, not a statement about origins.
3: pleionos gar doxēs houtos para Mōysēn ēxiōtai, kath' hoson pleiona timēn echei tou oikou ho kataskeuasas auton — "But Jesus has been found worthy of greater honor than Moses — just as the builder of a house deserves more honor than the house itself" (ēxiōtai: perfect passive of axioō: to account worthy — permanently counted as worthy): the comparison is structural: builder > house in honor. Moses is part of the house; the Son built it. Pleionos doxēs (more glory — genitive of comparison with pleionos): the comparison is about degree of glory, not denial of Moses's glory; the greater/lesser structure is the rhetorical engine of Hebrews throughout.
4: pas gar oikos kataskeuazetai hypo tinos, ho de panta kataskeuasas theos — "Every house is built by someone, but God is the builder of everything" (kataskeuazetai hypo tinos: is constructed by someone — passive, with a builder implied): the premise is universal (every house requires a builder) and leads to the inference that if the Son built the house (v3), he participates in the divine building role, since all building ultimately traces back to God. The verse grounds the Son's superiority to Moses in the Son's participation in divine creative agency.
5: kai Mōysēs men pistos en holō tō oikō autou hōs therapōn, eis martyrion tōn lalēthēsomenōn — "Moses was faithful as a servant in all God's house, giving testimony to what would be said in the future" (therapōn: servant, attendant — G2324, a word of dignity; not the ordinary doulos [slave] but a personal attendant in a position of honor; used of Moses only in the LXX [Num 12:7]): the servant-position is honorable but subordinate. Eis martyrion tōn lalēthēsomenōn (for a testimony of the things that would be spoken): Moses's entire ministry was anticipatory — a forward-pointing witness to the realities that would be spoken in the Son.
7: dio kathōs legei to pneuma to hagion, Sēmeron ean tēs phōnēs autou akousēte — "So, as the Holy Spirit says: Today, if you hear his voice," (Ps 95:7-8 LXX): the attribution to to pneuma to hagion (the Holy Spirit) as the one who speaks (legei — present tense) makes the Psalm's address present and ongoing: the Spirit continues to speak this word to the community. Sēmeron (today) — the Psalm's urgent temporal marker that Hebrews will develop into the "today" of the community's hearing moment.
8: mē sklērynēte tas kardias hymōn hōs en tō parapikrasmō, kata tēn hēmeran tou peirasmou en tē erēmō — "do not harden your hearts as you did in the rebellion, on the day of testing in the wilderness" (sklērynēte: aorist subjunctive of sklērunō: to harden, to make stiff/callous — the aorist expresses a completed act of hardening): parapikrasmos (rebellion, embitterment — G3894, from pikraino: to make bitter; the prefix para adds directional force — a bitter opposition against God): the LXX translates the place-names Meribah and Massah as events (rebellion/testing) rather than preserving the Hebrew toponyms. The warning is against the internal act of hardening — a volitional stiffening against the voice.
9: hou epeirasan hoi pateres hymōn en dokimasia kai eidon ta erga mou tessarakonta etē — "where your ancestors tested and tried me, though they saw my works for forty years" (epeirasan: tested — from peirazō: to test, to put to proof; dokimasia: testing/proving — here in apposition, intensifying): the paradox is striking: forty years of seeing the works and yet testing the worker. The sustained duration of the wilderness miracles makes the continued testing inexcusable. The "testing" of God is the inverse of proper faith: treating God as unproven rather than trusting the demonstrated record.
10: dio prosōchthisa tē genea tautē kai eipon, Aei planōntai tē kardia, autoi de ouk egnōsan tas hodous mou — "That is why I was angry with that generation; I said, Their hearts are always going astray, and they have not known my ways" (prosōchthisa: I was disgusted/wearied with — from prosochtizō: a strong word for angry disgust, being fed up; the divine emotional response to sustained rebellion): aei planōntai (always they go astray — present tense: a continuous wandering): the heart-wandering is habitual, not occasional. Ouk egnōsan tas hodous mou (they did not know my ways): egnōsan — the knowing is relational/experiential (not merely intellectual) knowledge of how God works; they experienced the ways but refused to recognize them.
11: hōs ōmosa en tē orgē mou, Ei eiseleusontai eis tēn katapausin mou — "So I swore in my anger, They shall never enter my rest" (Ps 95:11 LXX): ōmosa en tē orgē mou (I swore in my anger): the divine oath is made at the height of provocation, making it immovable. The Greek construction Ei eiseleusontai translates a Hebrew oath formula (ʾim + imperfect) that means "they shall absolutely not enter" — the "if" is not conditional but a strong negative assertion carried by the oath. Katapausin mou (my rest) — the rest is God's own rest, the eschatological Sabbath rest that the wilderness generation forfeited.
12: blepete, adelphoi, mēpote estai en tini hymōn kardia ponēra apistias en tō apostēnai apo theou zōntos — "See to it, brothers and sisters, that none of you has a sinful, unbelieving heart that turns away from the living God" (blepete: watch/see carefully — present imperative, ongoing vigilance required): kardia ponēra apistias (an evil heart of unbelief — ponēra: wicked, harmful; apistias: genitive of apistia, unbelief): the evil heart is not characterized by gross sin but by the specific evil of apistia — failure to trust. Apostēnai apo theou zōntos (to fall away from the living God — apistanai: to stand away from, to defect; the word behind "apostasy"): the departure is directional — away from the living God. Theou zōntos (living God) — the life of God makes his claims binding in a way that an inert idol cannot sustain.
15: en tō legesthai, Sēmeron ean tēs phōnēs autou akousēte, mē sklērynēte tas kardias hymōn hōs en tō parapikrasmō — "As it has just been said: Today, if you hear his voice, do not harden your hearts as you did in the rebellion" (Ps 95:7-8, repeated): the repetition of the Psalm citation (cf. vv7-8) within the same argument is rhetorical — the "today" is held open by the repeated utterance. Each reiteration of the warning extends the window of opportunity. The structure of the Greek conditional (ean + subjunctive) is the third-class condition: the hearing is genuinely possible but not guaranteed.
16: tines gar akousantes parepikranan? all' ou pantes hoi exelthontes ex Aigyptou dia Mōyseōs — "Who were they who heard and rebelled? Were they not all those Moses led out of Egypt?" (parepikranan: they rebelled, made bitter — aorist indicative): the rhetorical question format drives the analysis of the wilderness failure. The answer is uncomfortable: it was not a fringe minority but the entire generation (all' ou pantes — "was it not all of them?") who had witnessed the exodus. The precedent for the warning is the majority failure of a privileged community — not outsiders who never heard.
17: tisi de prosōchthisen tessarakonta etē; ouchi tois hamartēsasin, hōn ta kōla epesen en tē erēmō — "And with whom was God angry for forty years? Was it not with those who sinned, whose bodies perished in the wilderness?" (ta kōla: the limbs, the corpses — G2966, originally meaning "limbs" but used for whole corpses [LXX Num 14:29]): the duration (tessarakonta etē, forty years) appears again; the sustained divine displeasure was matched by the long wilderness sojourn. The physical death — bodies falling in the wilderness — is the external evidence of God's oath enacted.
18: tisi de ōmosen mē eiseleusesthai eis tēn katapausin autou ei mē tois apeithēsasin — "And to whom did God swear that they would never enter his rest if not to those who disobeyed?" (apeithēsasin: those who disobeyed — dative plural aorist participle of apeitheō: to refuse to be persuaded, to disobey): Hebrews uses apeitheō (disobedience) as the interpretive gloss for the wilderness failure — the same root as apistia (unbelief, vv12,19): unbelief and disobedience are the same failure seen from different angles. One who refuses to trust God refuses to obey God.
19: kai blepomen hoti ouk ēdynēthēsan eiselthein di' apistian — "So we see that they were unable to enter because of their unbelief" (ouk ēdynēthēsan: they were unable — not an external barrier but the incapacity produced by apistia): di' apistian (because of unbelief — the causal dia + accusative): unbelief is not merely a symptom but the operative cause of exclusion. The summary verse gathers the entire Ps 95 exposition: hardening → rebellion → exclusion, all rooted in the single failure of apistia. The connection with the following chapter is seamless: if apistia excluded them, only pistis can ensure entry.
